entry.title: Re: Estimated Cost should not be released if BOM is not d...
entry.content: In Public Cloud, you cannot freely create new costing variants, but you can work with the delivered ones and adjust their settings. The key is the valuation strategy sequence inside the costing variant. By removing fallback options such as “material master price” or “purchasing info record,” and leaving only BOM explosion in the sequence, the system will require a BOM for costing. If a BOM is missing, the costing run will stop with an error instead of releasing a cost estimate.

entry.title: Re: Scope items for Contract and Lease Management in Publ...
entry.content:
Hi Jeroen,
using scope item Real Estate Cost Settlement (6N8) requires architecture object information. With that, an integration to SAP Real Estate and Facilities Management by Planon is required.
Scope item Intercompany Lease Accounting – Group Ledger IFRS (7G4) can be used with or without integration to SAP Real Estate and Facilities Management by Planon. In case you want to use it without integration to SAP Real Estate and Facilities Management by Planon please make sure, that the intercompany rentable und usable objects have the correct usage category (I4 - Intercompany Rentable Object w/o Portfolio Structure, I2 - Intercompany Usable Object w/o Portfolio Structure). See also Generic Rental Objects | SAP Help Portal

Kind regards,
Anja

entry.title: Issue in Creating SES with Multiple Subline Items Due to Decimal Quantity Limitation
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We are facing an issue while creating the Service Entry Sheet (SES) for a Purchase Order that was migrated from ECC to Grow.The PO contains multiple subline items (approximately 1,000 lines). During SES creation, users are unable to enter the exact service quantities, as the system restricts quantity values to only three decimal places (e.g., 0.123). Due to this SAP system limitation, a shortfall in the total amount is occurring when compared to the PO value at the time of SES posting.Because of this discrepancy, users are unable to complete the SES with the precise quantity and value reflected in the PO.Could you please advise on the recommended approach to proceed with a trial or interim SES entry in such scenarios? Specifically, guidance would be appreciated on:

  • How to handle the quantity rounding differences
  • Whether an adjustment line or alternative process is recommended
  • Any best practices or SAP-supported workaround for SES creation with high-volume subline items

Your support and suggestions on how to proceed in such cases would be greatly appreciated.Thank you for your assistance.

Additionally, could you please confirm whether there is any possibility to import SES service line items via a file (e.g., Excel/upload template or mass upload option) instead of manual entry, especially for POs with a high number of service subline items?

Regards,

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Issue in Creating SES with Multiple Subline Items Due...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for the detailed explanation and for outlining the challenges so clearly — I understand how this situation, especially with a high number of service lines, can impact your SES processing.

Please find below our guidance:

1. Decimal Limitation (System Behavior)

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, quantities are maintained with a maximum of 3 decimal places as part of the standard system design.

Because of this limitation, it is expected that minor rounding differences may occur between the Purchase Order and the Service Entry Sheet values when higher precision quantities are involved. At present, this precision cannot be extended.

2. Handling the Rounding Difference

To manage this discrepancy, the following approach is recommended:

  • Adjustment Line in SES (Recommended)
    You can add an additional line item in the SES to adjust the small difference in value caused by rounding. This is the most practical and commonly followed approach in such scenarios.

  • Distributing the Difference
    Alternatively, small adjustments can be spread across multiple lines, though this may be less convenient given the volume of items.

  • Final SES Balancing
    Any remaining minor variance can also be handled in the final SES to ensure alignment with the PO value.

3. Handling Large Number of Service Lines

We understand that managing ~1000 sublines can be time-consuming. While the system supports it, such volume can make processing more complex.

For future cases, where possible, you may consider:

  • Grouping or consolidating service lines

  • Reducing excessive granularity in the PO structure

4. Mass Upload / Excel Import Option

Regarding your question on uploading SES via file:

  • The transaction ML87 and program MM_PUR_SES_UPLOAD are not available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, as they belong to legacy/on-premise functionality.

  • https://me.sap.com/notes/3383958/E

Best regards,
Aishwarya

entry.title: Mastering Test Management & Automation for SAP Digital Manufacturing: Webinar series with concircle
entry.content:
As SAP Digital Manufacturing landscapes continue to evolve, organizations are unlocking new levels of innovation, scalability, and integration across their operations.

At the same time, this evolution brings new requirements for how testing is approached:

  • Frequent releases bring continuous innovation
    Regular updates create opportunities to deliver value faster, while requiring structured regression testing to ensure consistency and reliability.
  • Manufacturing processes are business-critical
    Ensuring quality and stability across shop floor operations remains a top priority.
  • System landscapes are becoming more interconnected
    Integrations, custom extensions, and multi-plant environments call for a more structured and scalable testing approach.
  • Manual testing does not scale
    Organizations are increasingly moving from manual execution towards more efficient, automated, and transparent testing practices.
  • End-to-end visibility becomes more and more important
    Connecting processes, risks, and test cases helps teams make better, more informed testing decisions.

As a result, many organizations are now focusing on how to scale their testing capabilities while maintaining reliability and supporting continuous innovation.

What you will learn

In this webinar series, we will walk you through a structured approach to test management and automation for SAP Digital Manufacturing, from foundation to advanced practices:

Session 1 – Test Management in SAP Cloud ALM for SAP Digital Manufacturing

Tuesday, April 28, 2026
9:00 AM – 10:00 AM EDT
3:00 PM - 4:00 PM CEST

Get an overview of Test Management in SAP Cloud ALM for SAP Digital Manufacturing, from building a strong testing foundation to enabling scalable automation. Gain real-world insights from partner concircle on risk-driven, process-based testing.

Session 2 – Integration & Automation with SAP Test Automation by Tricentis

Thursday, April 30, 2026
9:00 AM – 10:00 AM EDT
3:00 PM - 4:00 PM CEST

Explore the integration of SAP Cloud ALM and SAP Test Automation by Tricentis for SAP Digital Manufacturing. This session also includes practical guidance from partner concircle on getting started with test automation and key prerequisites for success.

Session 3 – End-to-End Test Automation for SAP Digital Manufacturing

Tuesday, May 5, 2026
9:00 AM – 10:00 AM EDT
3:00 PM - 4:00 PM CEST

Gain insights into an end-to-end test automation approach for SAP Digital Manufacturing with partner concircle — combining process, risk, and execution. Learn how to reduce test effort, improve quality, manage quarterly releases, and generate automated test cases using Tosca (TTA/ECT).

Session 4 – Advanced Test Automation Engineering for SAP Digital Manufacturing

Thursday, May 7, 2026
9:00 AM – 10:00 AM EDT
3:00 PM - 4:00 PM CEST

Deep dive into advanced test automation engineering for SAP Digital Manufacturing, focusing on scalable, maintainable, and robust automation approaches. Learn how to structure automation using reusable components and clear architectures, and apply best practices for configuration handling, execution stability, test data management, and scaling with Tosca Cloud.

Join us

Whether you are just starting with test management or already exploring automation, this series is designed to support you at different stages of your journey 👉 Register now and secure your spot.

We look forward to having you join us!

 

Feedback or questions? 

We continuously evolve our enablement offerings based on feedback from customers and partners. If you have questions, are looking for specific resources, or would like to suggest topics for future webcasts, feel free to reach out to us at: engage-manufacturing@sap.com.  We’d be happy to hear your thoughts and continuously improve our content based on your input.

entry.title: Introducing Powerful Innovations for System Administrators in Our H1 2026 Release
entry.content:
At SAP, we deeply appreciate the critical role system administrators and HR IT teams play in keeping HR operations resilient, compliant, and continuously improving. With our H1 2026 release, we’re investing in capabilities that make your work faster, safer, and easier—so you can focus on delivering value. This release advances integration, extensibility, and compliance with three standout highlights.

Embedded Extensibility Wizard: Build the Right Extensions, Faster
We’re bringing the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) extensibility wizard directly into SAP SuccessFactors solutions. This guided, in-app experience walks you through creating custom extensions—whether that’s a new data field, a People Profile tile for custom or external data, or an end-to-end business process—and ensures they’re seamlessly integrated with your SAP SuccessFactors landscape. Specifically, you can extend the People Profile, Spotlight, or Home Page by creating new tiles (UI cards) that surface organization-specific data from SuccessFactors or embed SAP BTP extensions, so users see all the context they need in one place.

  • What it means for you: Extend the People Profile with new tiles to surface unique HR or external data and accelerate delivery while aligning HR processes to your unique requirements.
  • Why it matters: Bring custom and external data into People Profile in a standardized, governed way—reducing complexity and manual effort and boosting productivity across HR and IT teams.
  • Real-world impact: Give HR, managers, and employees a richer, holistic view in People Profile while streamlining operations with extensions that fit your business today and scale with you tomorrow. 

New API for the SAP Audit Log Service: Strengthen Compliance and Risk Management
SAP SuccessFactors HCM already exposes audit data to the SAP Audit Log service. With this release, we’re introducing a new API that programmatically surfaces read audit, change audit, and general audit data.

  • What it means for you: Access audit logs via the new interface or continue to use the SAP Audit Log Viewer UI—whichever fits your workflow.
  • Why it matters: Integrate SAP SuccessFactors audit logs with your internal GRC or SIEM systems to enhance monitoring, automate evidence collection, and speed audit responses.
  • Real-world impact: Improve compliance oversight, reduce manual effort and costs, and increase operational efficiency with direct, secure access to the data you need.

New AI Agents for Permissions and Transport: Revolutionize Your System Administration
As part of our SAP-wide AI innovation program, we’re introducing two AI-powered agents (availability planned for June) to help streamline system administration tasks. We picked two of the most critical and time-consuming activities.

  • Configuration Transport Agent: Quickly finds configurations, automatically bundles recent changes, and exports transport bundles to simplify and de-risk rollouts across your landscape. This helps reduce errors in a critical process and supports stronger business continuity.
  • Role-Based Permissions Agent: Accelerates troubleshooting by diagnosing permission configuration issues and recommending actionable fixes with impact analysis. This can reduce resolution times from hours to minutes—without requiring specialized tools or deep-dive expertise.

Together, these H1 2026 enhancements represent a significant step forward in administrative efficiency and AI-powered assistance. The combination of streamlined extensibility, enhanced audit capabilities, and intelligent agents positions your organization for more agile, secure, and efficient HR operations.

The focus on reducing administrative overhead while improving security and compliance capabilities means you can spend more time on strategic initiatives and less time on routine maintenance tasks.

Get Started and Learn More
To dive deeper into these and other enhancements, visit the Product Release & Road Map page in the SAP SuccessFactors Community. For an overview of the release, check out the release highlights brochure and videos. For details, go to the What’s New Viewer. We can’t wait for you to experience how these updates streamline your day-to-day work and strengthen your HR foundation for the long term.

entry.title: Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Fit-to-Standard Workshops
entry.content:
Introduction


This blog is part of my small blog series Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
In this post, we’re moving into the Explore phase of the SAP Activate methodology.

The Explore phase is the critical third phase of SAP Activate where teams perform fit-to-standard analysis to validate that SAP's solution functionality can satisfy business requirements. This phase conducts structured workshops to review SAP best practice functionality, identify delta requirements, and document the conceptual design of the to-be delivered solution.

More details for Scoping you will find in SAP Activate via Road Map Viewer Road Map Viewer - SAP for Me
Navigation: Tab 'Content' -> Phase 'Explore' -> 'Fit-to-Standard Workshops'

 

Workshop Execution

Based on most relevant Scope Items for Business Partner you execute the standard processes covered during the Fit-to-Standard workshops. The SAP Best Practices content, test scripts, and process flows that were used by the Configuration Expert for the demonstration are used.

Accounts Receivable (J59)
Accounts Payable (J60)
Mass Load and Mass Maintenance for Business Partners (7MI)

 

Little Helpers

Workshop Slides

As a foundation for your Business Partner Fit-to-Standard workshop you can use this slides

English version



German version

 

As you can see, I use already Business Partner like an own scope item related to scope items mentioned above.

 

Test Scripts for Demo

If you like you can step into customers starter system which has proper demo data already.
As a guideline I recommend to use test scripts for master data provided by SAP.

Create Customer Master - MDS (BND) 

Create Supplier Master - MDS (BNE) 

Please pay attention to version field at top of the page. It might be that you have to adjust it.

 

Move from SAP Signavio to SAP CALM

At that point where you move forward from SAP Signavio to CALM (SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management) consider to create a generic Business Partner requirement in CALM in addition to Customer and Supplier requirements.

This approach will help to collect all Business Partner relevant information at one central point. I know this is not the standard way but helpfull.

 


Conclusion

You have successfully validated Business Partner processes against SAP standard functionality through structured demonstrations using starter system with demo data. Now you are ready to document all delta requirements in SAP Cloud ALM, creating a prioritized backlog while maintaining the cloud mindset principle of minimal customization.

Next steps are now the transition from SAP Signavio Process Navigator to CALM (SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management) with the innovative approach of creating a generic Business Partner requirement alongside Customer and Supplier requirements.

entry.title: Re: How to dynamically calculate IT0014 wage type (20% of...
Story Reports: Data Export in View Mode
entry.content:
Dear all,

With the H1 2026 release, a new feature - Data Export in View Mode for Story Reports | SAP Help Portal - will be introduced. This feature allows users with view-only access to Story Reports to download the data from the underlying data sources of the report.

While I understand the intention behind this feature, it poses a significant risk of granting overly broad access to underlying data, including the ability to download it.

There are several scenarios in which we deliberately provide only aggregated reports. In these cases, users are intended to see aggregated data only and explicitly not the raw, row-level data behind the reports — and certainly not be able to download it.

I believe this feature can be valuable for certain users and roles. However, it should be secured either through a dedicated permission or a configurable option within Story Reports.

If the feature remains unchanged for the H1 2026 rollout in May, I am concerned that we will be forced to remove many reports from user self-service, which would represent a significant setback.

Thank you and kind regards,
Sebastian

entry.title: Re: Access Control in ABAP CDS Using User Defined Aspects
entry.content:
@NooruBohra 

Thanks for checking this and for adding the ST05 trace—that really helps make the behavior more visible. 

You’re right in what you observed. In cases where pure user-defined aspects are used (without any PFCG-based checks), STAUTHTRACE may not always capture entries, because it primarily logs classical  authority-check executions tied to authorization objects.

In CDS access control, especially with user-defined aspects, the filtering happens more at the data layer (SQL generation level) rather than through explicit authorization checks. That’s why ST05 becomes the most reliable tool to understand how the WHERE clause is expanded.

That said, I’ve seen in some mixed scenarios (for example, when aspects are combined with PFCG roles or standard authorization objects), STAUTHTRACE can still be useful to validate the authorization part of the flow.

So maybe a simple way to position it is:

  • ST05 → best for understanding data filtering (actual SQL)
  • STAUTHTRACE → useful only when authorization objects / roles are involved

Also, your example nicely highlights another important point—the “no data” case when the aspect returns nothing. This is something many developers miss during testing.

Overall, great addition to the blog. It’s becoming very practical for developers.  

Thx.

entry.title: Re: MRP Settings - MDBT creating Planned Orders then Purc...
entry.content:
When MRP results flip between planned orders and purchase requisitions, it usually points to a configuration or master data nuance rather than a pure system bug. Here are the main areas you should investigate:

1) Procurement type in material master
If the material is set to “E” (in‑house production) in one plant and “F” (external procurement) in another, MRP will generate different proposals. Cross‑plant differences can cause the weekend run to overwrite the daily run’s results.

2) MRP control parameters in the planning run
Daily run (NETCH) vs. weekly run (NETPL) use different logic. NETPL re‑plans only within the planning horizon, while NETCH re‑plans all net changes. The “Create purchase requisitions” setting (2 vs. 3) determines whether MRP creates requisitions directly or planned orders first. If the weekly run uses “2” (direct PR creation) and the daily run uses “3” (planned orders), the system will keep flipping.

3) Planning file entries
Even though you confirmed the material is in the planning file, check whether the planning horizon and rescheduling parameters differ between plants.

4) Lot sizing and procurement type
Lot‑sizing procedures (e.g., FX vs. EX) and procurement type inconsistencies can cause MRP to regenerate different order types.

5) Scheduling agreements or quota arrangements
If active, they can override the default proposal type.

entry.title: First Half 2026 Release: What’s new in SAP SuccessFactors solutions for workforce management
entry.content:
The first half 2026 release of SAP SuccessFactors solutions for workforce management brings a wave of innovation across time tracking, absence management, and workforce scheduling. From AI-powered pay transparency to intelligent shift planning, these updates help organizations manage their workforce with greater precision, compliance, and efficiency. In this blog, I’ll walk through the key highlights across SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking, Employee Central Time Management, and Workforce Scheduling. For a full overview, be sure to check out the What’s New Viewer.

Closing the loop between time valuation and pay transparency

One of the standout additions in this release is the integration of time sheet data into the Payroll Explanation Agent. Employees can now receive clear, step-by-step explanations of how their recorded working time, overtime, and premiums are valued and how these valuations directly affect their pay. By bringing together time and payroll data into a single natural language experience, the agent removes the complexity behind pay calculations and puts actionable insight directly in the hands of employees.

This capability strengthens trust between employees and their organizations. Rather than relying on HR to interpret payslips or resolve time-and-pay discrepancies, employees get fast, accurate answers about how their time entries translate into earnings. The result is less administrative overhead for HR and a more informed, empowered workforce.

Payroll Explanation Agent with time sheet data

Strengthening compliance and overtime control

This release also introduces attendance quotas, giving organizations tighter control of overtime recording. Employees can record overtime or other quotas within predefined, approved limits, and the system automatically handles situations where no pre-approval exists, whether that means triggering alerts or posting automatically to flextime accounts.

For organizations managing complex labor policies or operating in regulated industries, this capability helps prevent unauthorized overtime postings, supports budget control, and ensures that time recording remains compliant without requiring constant manual oversight. Read more here: Managing Pre-approvals using Attendance Quota

Attendance quotas

Resolving time conflicts automatically

Managing overlaps between absences and attendances has long been a time-consuming task for time administrators. The enhanced collision handling capability in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking addresses this directly. When a collision is detected, the system automatically resolves the overlap and surfaces a clear warning message on the time sheet, so employees can verify and take action if needed.

This update provides greater flexibility with enhanced collision configuration options, eliminates the need for manual record adjustments in situations where corrections would otherwise be overwhelming, and supports compliance with regulatory requirements. Read more here: Collision Handling between Absences and Attendances in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking

Collision handling

Expanding payroll integration options

For organizations using external payroll providers, this release introduces structured guidance and tooling for 3rd party payroll integration with SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking. This includes best practices for extracting, formatting, and transmitting time tracking data, including absences, time valuation results, payouts, and time accounts, to external payroll systems.

The integration leverages a new API for replication confirmation to third-party payroll systems, ensuring accurate handling of retroactive changes. The outcome is enhanced payroll accuracy, reduced maintenance effort, and stronger compliance with organizational and regulatory requirements. Read more here: Extending Data Replication Proxies to Third-Party Payroll Systems

3rd Party Payroll Integration

Advancing time recording for public sector

For customers running SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking alongside SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition, this release delivers consolidated time recording enhancements for the public sector. These updates provide access to actual costs based on time valuation results within funds and grants management reporting, addressing a critical requirement for regulated public sector organizations.

Employees can now record time entries using alternative cost objects such as funds, grants, fund centers, and WBS elements, not just cost centers. This enables regulated industries to report actual costs rather than planned costs using payroll-processed time data, which is essential for compliance with funds and grants management regulations.

Deeper absence insights with People Intelligence

This release brings a dedicated Time Management Insights app as part of People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud. HR teams now have a single, comprehensive view of workforce absence analytics, including absence duration and occurrence data, unscheduled absence and sick leave tracking, and absence rates with contributing factors.

By identifying absence patterns and trends early, organizations can take proactive action to reduce absenteeism, improve resource planning, and evaluate the impact of health and wellness initiatives. This capability enables both internal benchmarking and external comparisons, giving HR leaders the data they need to strengthen overall workforce strategy.

Time Management Insights

SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Scheduling

Following its early adopter launch in December 2025, SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Scheduling is planned for general availability this July. This release delivers two foundational capabilities that bring workforce planning and execution together in a single, streamlined experience.

Demand definition allows planners to define labor needs by work center and shift, specifying required headcount, skills, and utilization factors. A consolidated view of planned headcount, planned hours, and total shifts gives planners complete visibility into coverage requirements, with mass edit capabilities and full customization for each shift and work center.

The new shift scheduling board with shift proposals takes this further by generating system-driven shift proposals based on the defined demand. These proposals factor in required skills, employee absences, public holidays, and planned working hours, allowing planners to validate and publish more efficiently.

New shift board (lab preview)

 

Together, these features help organizations right-size coverage, protect service levels, control labor costs, and keep schedules aligned and compliant across locations. For manufacturing and production environments where scheduling precision directly impacts output, this is a significant step forward.

Explore What’s New

You can explore all the new enhancements for SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking, Employee Central Time Management. Information about SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Scheduling will be available at the planned GA in July 2026. For additional release information and assets, visit the SAP SuccessFactors community page.

entry.title: Re: Licensing of decommissioning SAP application
entry.content:

entry.title: Re: Storage Location Not Defaulting in MIGO for Movement...
entry.content:
Dear @Premium,

In Public Cloud this is standard system behavior. Movement types 103 and 105 are part of the two-step GR blocked stock process so the storage location is not handled in the same way as in a standard unrestricted-use goods receipt. There is no documented Public Cloud SSCUI in CBC to force the purchase-order storage location to default into the GR screen for this 103/105 process

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003459964

Summary:

This should be positioned as standard behavior, not as a missing PO setting or configuration gap.

Additional Information:

I tried to use Badi = MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA and force the value in SL to MIGO after selecting PO but SAP does not allow such updates

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Fit-to-Standard Workshop Documentation
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog is part of my small blog series Business Partner @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
With reference to the SAP Activate methodology, we are still in the explore phase now.

More details for Scoping you will find in SAP Activate via Road Map Viewer Road Map Viewer - SAP for Me
Navigation: Tab 'Content' -> Phase 'Explore' -> 'Fit-to-Standard Workshops Documentation'

 

Record Workshop findings

Now that we've wrapped up our Business Partner fit-to-standard workshops, it's time to get organized with our findings. Think of this documentation phase as creating your project's "memory bank" – everything we discovered, validated, and decided needs to be captured systematically so nothing gets lost in translation when we move to the Realize phase.
The beauty of proper documentation is that it becomes your single source of truth. No more "I think we discussed this in the workshop" conversations – everything's documented, prioritized, and ready for action.

As mentioned in previous blogs already, create you own requirement for Business Partner in SAP Cloud ALM to have a central store.

As in On-Prem or Private Cloud environment customer and supplier master data has a strong relationship to Account Group requirements coming from all other streams along the project. In Public Cloud as well Account Groups will keep their importance to group customer and supplier master data, for field control, partner determination, text determination, …..

Below you will have access to an Excel sheet which in my eyes is very helpful to collect all required Account Groups. This Excel sheet will work as your main design document to decide which Account Groups and number ranges can be reused and which you have to create and map.


This Excel sheet provides standard configuration data that is included out-of-the-box, allowing you to understand the system's default setup.


Conclusion

All workshop findings systematically captured in SAP Cloud ALM with requirements documented separately for granular tracking and resolution now. Let's go for the next step Data Load Preparation.

entry.title: How Advanced Predictive Planning Is Reshaping Modern Business
entry.content:
In a world where markets shift overnight and expectations evolve faster than planning cycles can keep up, organizations are increasingly looking for a new way to forecast, plan, and adapt. Traditional planning models—built on historic spreadsheets, manual adjustments, and lengthy consolidation periods—are no longer enough. Businesses need the ability to look ahead, respond quickly, and make decisions with confidence. 

This is where advanced predictive planning in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) has changed the game. By bringing embedded machine learning and predictive analytics directly into the planning process, SAC enables organizations to move from reactive planning to proactive, insight-driven forecasting. Instead of spending hours wrestling with outdated files or reconciling conflicting numbers, teams can now generate accurate predictions, run simulations, and understand the impact of decisions long before taking action. 

At its core, predictive planning is about using historical data and patterns to anticipate what might happen next. SAC does this by analyzing years of company data, identifying trends, and automatically generating forecasts based on machine learning models. But what makes SAC’s approach so powerful is how seamlessly this intelligence integrates into existing planning workflows. There is no separate model to manage and no technical expertise required. Planners simply run predictive forecasting within their familiar environment, and SAC produces automated, statistically sound projections. 

For many organizations, this shift immediately improves planning accuracy. Machine learning models are not influenced by human bias, guesswork, or inconsistent manual inputs. They evaluate underlying trends, detect seasonality, and adapt to changes buried deep within the data. This means forecasts reflect what the business is actually experiencing—not just what planners hope or expect to happen. And because the predictions update in real time as data changes, decision-makers always work with the most accurate picture of the future. 

But predictive planning goes far beyond improving the numbers. It also reshapes the decision-making process itself. When planners can instantly simulate different scenarios—raising investments here, reducing costs there, adjusting assumptions for market volatility or supply chain constraints—they are able to explore options that were previously too time-consuming to analyze. Leadership conversations become more dynamic because leaders can test ideas live, adjust plans on the spot, and immediately see how choices impact performance. 

Scenario modeling has quickly become one of the most valuable aspects of SAC’s predictive capabilities. Instead of treating planning as a once-a-year event, organizations can now evaluate a range of possible futures. What happens if demand spikes? What if it slows? How would staffing, budgets, or supply chain needs change under different conditions? SAC provides the models that help businesses answer these questions with confidence. And because the simulations are automated, teams can test dozens of scenarios without the burden of manual recalculations. 

This level of agility is crucial in uncertain environments. A sudden market shift, a regulatory change, or disruptions in operations can throw off even the most carefully structured plan. Predictive planning gives companies the ability to adapt quickly. When something changes, plans can be updated immediately, simulations rerun, and decisions realigned—often in the same meeting. It is this combination of automation, speed, and intelligence that transforms planning from a rigid process into a flexible, real-time capability. 

Another strength of SAC’s predictive planning is how well it connects different parts of the business. Planning is rarely just a finance exercise. Sales forecasts impact operations, headcount planning shapes budgets, and marketing investment decisions influence pipeline projections. SAC centralizes these inputs, ensuring that predictive calculations draw from a unified source of data rather than scattered spreadsheets. This creates a shared planning environment where teams have consistent visibility and where cross-functional alignment happens naturally. 

The automation of forecasting and simulation also reduces the amount of manual work planners need to perform. Instead of spending time updating spreadsheets or reconciling data, teams can focus on interpreting insights and shaping strategy. This shift elevates the role of planning professionals from data manipulators to strategic partners. It also increases trust across the organization, as predictions and scenarios are grounded in transparent, statistically rigorous models rather than assumptions or outdated figures. 

Perhaps the most compelling outcome is the confidence predictive planning provides. Business leaders can make decisions knowing they are backed by real data, real trends, and realistic scenarios. They don’t have to rely on intuition alone. They can explore alternatives without delay. And they can plan with full awareness of how choices ripple through the business. 

As organizations move toward more dynamic, integrated, and intelligent planning processes, SAC’s predictive planning capabilities will continue to grow in importance. The ability to foresee outcomes, simulate change, and adapt fast is becoming a competitive differentiator. Companies that embrace predictive planning are not just improving accuracy—they’re transforming planning into a strategic advantage. 

In a world defined by change, the future belongs to businesses that can anticipate it. With advanced predictive planning and simulation in SAP Analytics Cloud, that future becomes clearer, faster, and far more within reach.

entry.title: Re: BADI ME_PROCESS_OUT_CUST Error
entry.content:
Hello!

This is a very common issue when working with MM BAdIs like ME_PROCESS_OUT_CUST or ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST. You are completely right in your observation: the SET_DATA method is the trigger for the loop.

The Root Cause

When you call SET_DATA, you are telling the standard SAP program that the document data has been modified. To ensure overall data consistency, the SAP framework automatically re-triggers the BAdI to re-validate this new state.

If your code unconditionally assigns your custom values and calls SET_DATA every single time the BAdI runs, it creates an infinite loop. To protect the system from hanging, SAP has a hardcoded safeguard that aborts the process after 10 iterations, throwing the MEOUT - 134 error you are seeing.

The Solution: Conditional Updates

The standard best practice to save custom field data without triggering this loop is to compare the values before setting them. You must only call SET_DATA if your custom logic actually introduces a change to the existing data.

Here is how you should structure your code:

 
  DATA: ls_item    TYPE meout_item,
        lv_changed TYPE abap_bool.

  " 1. Get the current data from the framework
  ls_item = im_item->get_data( ).

  " 2. Compare the current value with your new calculated value
  " (Replace 'zz_custom_field' and 'lv_new_value' with your actual logic)
  IF ls_item-zz_custom_field <> lv_new_value.
    ls_item-zz_custom_field = lv_new_value.
    lv_changed = abap_true.  " Flag that a real change occurred
  ENDIF.

  " 3. ONLY execute SET_DATA if a modification was actually made
  IF lv_changed = abap_true.
    im_item->set_data( ls_item ).
  ENDIF.

Applies to Header and Items

It is worth noting that this exact same principle applies to the PROCESS_HEADER method as well. Whenever you are updating header data, you must extract it using im_header->get_data( ), check for real differences, and only trigger im_header->set_data( ) if the values don't match.

entry.title: Re: Does Output Management support local printing?
entry.content: Define multiple printer rules in OPD (e.g., by user, plant, or company code) so that different users get different printers automatically. If truly dynamic selection is required, you would need a custom enhancement (e.g., BAdI for Output Parameter Determination) to incorporate SU01 printer or user input. For Fiori apps that only support Output Management, the standard approach is to design OPD rules to cover the different user locations rather than relying on the LOCL dialog.

entry.title: Saudi Arabia: GOSI Maternity Compensation – Why the “Obvious” Solution Doesn’t Work
entry.content:
Let’s Start with the Questions Everyone Is Asking

When the General Organization for Social Insurance (GOSI) introduced Maternity Compensation, two immediate questions come up in every discussion:

“If GOSI pays the employee… why don’t we just stop salary or reverse it?”
“And why is GOSI paying the employee directly, not the employer?”

Both questions are valid—and actually connected.

Why Does GOSI Pay the Employee Directly?

From a business perspective, many expect the payment to go to the employer first.
But the design from GOSI is intentional:

  • The benefit is meant to support the employee directly
  • It ensures the employee receives compensation without dependency on employer processing
  • It reduces the employer’s burden, but does not replace employer payroll processes automatically

Which creates the core challenge:

"Payroll still pays salary… while GOSI also pays the employee separately."

 

The Real Scenario Everyone Is Facing

Let’s look at what actually happens in companies:

  • An employee takes paid maternity leave, then after a few months receives compensation from GOSI.
Now the employer asks:

“How do I recover the salary I already paid?”

 

First Thought: “Let’s Just Reverse It”

Idea:

Replace paid maternity leave with unpaid leave (retroactively)

Why it sounds correct:
  • Aligns with the legal concept
  • Removes employer cost
  • Clean from a business perspective
Why it doesn’t really work:
  • SAP payroll will reopen past periods
  • Everything gets recalculated (even closed payroll)
  • Financial postings may change
  • Other wage types get impacted (not just maternity-related)

✔️So yes, it solves the business logic…
 ❎But breaks payroll stability

 

Second Thought: “Ok, Then Just Deduct It Later”

Idea:

Recover the amount through future payroll

Why we like it:
  • No retroactive impact
  • Keeps historical payroll unchanged
  • Easy to control
But here’s the catch:
  • We don’t know the exact GOSI amount
  • No clear rule on:
  • Basic vs Housing split

✔️So yes, it solves the technical issue…
❎But doesn’t solve the calculation ambiguity

 

Third Thought: “Can We Automate It Fully?”

Idea:

Build logic in payroll to calculate and distribute everything automatically

Why it’s attractive:
  • No manual work
  • Fully system-driven
  • Clean process
Why we didn’t go there (yet):
  • No confirmed formula from GOSI
-->To elaborate:

GOSI defines the compensation as 100% of the average insured salary over the last 12 months.
However, from a system perspective, this introduces multiple limitations:

  • The 12-month average may include periods where the employee worked for different employers
  • The current employer does not have visibility into salary or contribution data from previous companies
  • There is no integration/API providing this cross-employer historical data
  • Additionally, due to Personal Data Protection Law (PDPL) considerations, the employer does not have the authority to request or rely on the exact compensation amount received by the employee from GOSI
-->Which means:
Even if we automate the calculation in SAP, it would be based on incomplete or indirect data, and cannot be validated against the actual amount paid by GOSI
  • No integration/API to validate results
  • High risk of calculating the wrong amount

✔️So yes, it solves the automation need…
❎But risks compliance and accuracy

 

Fourth Thought: “What If We Handle It Correctly from the Start?”

Idea:

If the employee requests GOSI compensation during the same period, we directly record:

--> Unpaid maternity leave instead of paid leave

Why this is the best-case scenario:
  • No recovery required later
  • No retroactive changes
  • Payroll remains clean
  • Aligns with the GOSI concept
So… what’s still missing?
  • No visibility on actual GOSI compensation amount
  • No defined split between:
    • Basic Salary
    • Housing Allowance
  • Requires strong coordination:
    • HR
    • Payroll
    • Employee
  • Additionally, the employee may dispute the employer’s action based on Saudi Labor Law Article 151, especially if the expectation is to receive paid maternity leave from the employer

✔️ So yes, it solves the timing issue…
❎But introduces potential employee relations and legal interpretation risks, in addition to dependency on external clarity and internal discipline

 

So, What Did We Actually Do?

We stepped back and asked:

“What is the safest way to handle this—without breaking payroll or assuming unclear rules?”

--> That’s why we delivered this as a KBA (Knowledge-Based Approach)

 

Reference: SAP KBA

For detailed configuration steps and system behavior, please refer to the official SAP Knowledge Base Article: 🔗KBA 

This KBA provides:
  • Step-by-step implementation guidance
  • Detailed explanation of standard payroll behavior
  • Recommended approaches (IT0014, IT0045, IT2001)
  • Technical considerations for retroactivity and deductions

 

Why a KBA Instead of a Standard Solution?

Because:

  • GOSI doesn’t provide full calculation details
  • SAP doesn’t receive actual compensation values
  • Customers handle cases differently
  • Each scenario depends on timing and business decisions

--> A fixed solution would either:

  • Be too rigid
  • Or introduce incorrect calculations

--> A KBA provides:

  • Flexibility
  • Control
  • Safe implementation

 

Let’s Be Honest About the Options

Let’s look at each option realistically:

  • Unpaid Leave (Same Period)
    This is the cleanest approach from a process perspective, as it avoids any recovery later and keeps payroll aligned from the beginning. However, it depends heavily on early action and still suffers from unclear calculation rules.
  • Unpaid Leave (Retroactive)
    This can correct past payments and align with the legal concept, but it comes at a high cost—triggering retroactive payroll recalculations and impacting payroll stability.
  • Recurring Deduction (IT0014)
    A safe and practical method to recover the amount in future payroll periods. It is simple and controlled but relies on manual input since the exact calculation from GOSI is not available.
  • Loan (IT0045)
    Provides structured tracking and visibility of the remaining balance, making it suitable for financial control. However, it is more complex to manage and still depends on manual calculation.
  • Automation (PCR)
    This approach offers full automation and flexibility in distributing amounts across wage components. However, without a reliable calculation source from GOSI, it introduces a high risk of incorrect results.

 

The Hidden Problem: Net Pay & Arrears

Even with the best approach, another issue appears:

“What if the deduction is higher than the employee’s net salary?”

--> SAP will:

  • Deduct partially
  • Carry the remaining amount as arrears (claims)
  • Recover it in future payroll periods

--> Meaning:

  • Recovery becomes gradual
  • Not guaranteed in one cycle

 

The Big Missing Piece

Everything comes back to one point:

We still don’t have a clear answer from GOSI on:

  • Exact calculation method
  • Component distribution
  • Actual compensation value

 

So, What’s the Right Approach Today?

Not perfect but practical:

✅Handle correctly from the start when possible
✅Avoid retroactive payroll changes
✅Use forward recovery when needed
✅Keep logic flexible and controlled

 

Final Thought

This is one of those cases where:

The legal idea is simple…
But system reality is not.

Every solution we explored:

  • Solves one part of the problem
  • But not the full picture

--> That’s why flexibility not automation is the right approach for now

 

What We’re Waiting For

To move toward a standard solution, we need:

  • Clear calculation rules from GOSI
  • Integration or API availability
  • Standardized scenarios

 

Stay Updated

To stay informed about upcoming updates, enhancements, and discussions related to this topic, we recommend visiting the MENA SAP User Group space, where we will be sharing the latest developments and guidance.

entry.title: Re: SAC setVisible via Switch
entry.content:
So This is how I set it up.

Added a switch and 2 tables. I have not overlapped the tables just to show it clearly.

Kept the switch to off initially. Then manually hid the table in left outline panel which I want to hide when switch is off. In My case Table 1.

So Initial view is showing Table 2 and switch off.

Script on change event of the switch.

if (Switch_1.isOn()) 
{
		Table_1.setVisible(true);
        Table_2.setVisible(false);
    } else {
        Table_1.setVisible(false);
		Table_2.setVisible(true);
	}

This is how it works.

Switch off, Table 2 is visible as set initially.

When switch is set to on, Table 1 is visible and Table 2 is hidden.

If you overlap the table on each other you will get the effect of a single table changing view.

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Make deliver note as mandatory when posting in MIGO w...
entry.content:
Dear @Moon3,

The correct enhancement spot for validating Delivery Note in Post Goods Movement (MIGO) is Badi MMIM_HEAD_CHECK_DATA because this is the header-level validation Badi for that app. SAP also explicitly recommends using MMIM_HEAD_CHECK_DATA for validation

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/85a220ae29a745aab169b630791d0487.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3610497

I have prepared a ready-made code that works:

DATA ls_message TYPE if_mmim_head_check_data=>ts_messages.

IF invtrymgmtcallingappl = 'MIGO'.

  IF header-deliverydocumentbyvendor IS INITIAL.

    ls_message-messagetype = 'E'.

    ls_message-messagetext = 'Enter delivery note'.

    APPEND ls_message TO messages.

  ENDIF.

ENDIF.

HOWEVER this requirement cannot be restricted strictly to movement types 107 and 109 because the released Badi interface available in the system does not expose a movement type field for that header validation. In that case the validation can only be implemented at the overall MIGO / Post Goods Movement level

Below available fields:

Best Regards,

Dawid

You could try the rankBY API. Just type Chart_1.rankBy({}); Inside the curly braces press Ctrl Space and it will allow to populate options as below.

https://help.sap.com/doc/1639cb9ccaa54b2592224df577abe822/release/en-US/index.html#Chart_MrankBy

//Top 5
Chart_1.rankBy({rankOrder:RankOrder.Top,structureMember:"[Account_BestRunJ_sold].[parentId].&[Gross_Margin]",value:5});

 

 

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: IAS Proxy System: Is fixed group assignment possible without sending groups in the inbound payload?
entry.content:
IAS Proxy System: Is fixed group assignment possible without sending groups in the inbound payload?

Hi all,

we are working with SAP Identity Provisioning and Identity Authentication as a proxy system. Our client calls the proxy SCIM endpoint like this:

/ipsproxy/service/api/v1/scim/<system_id>/Users

Our user provisioning itself works fine, including custom attributes via proxy read/write transformations.

What we are trying to achieve now is this: A newly created user should automatically be assigned to one fixed group that already exists in IAS.

What makes this a bit confusing is that in standard provisioning from a source system to a target system, SAP documents group assignment via assignGroup, where groups are identified by ID in the target transformation. So for source-to-target provisioning, assigning groups by target group ID seems to be supported.

However, in our case we are not using a normal source-to-target provisioning flow, but an IAS proxy system.

We found SAP KBA 3520458: Provision user's group assignment in Identity Authentication IPS proxy system

The KBA suggests the following mappings:

Read transformation

{
  "sourcePath": "$.groups",
  "targetPath": "$.groups",
  "optional": true,
  "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true
}

Write transformation

{
  "sourcePath": "$.groups[*].value",
  "targetPath": "$.groups[?(@.value)]",
  "optional": true,
  "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true
}

Our understanding is:

  • In standard source-to-target provisioning, assignGroup by group ID is supported in the target transformation.
  • In proxy mode, the KBA mapping seems to only pass through groups if they are already present in the inbound payload.
  • That would mean the calling system must send the IAS group ID in groups[*].value.

So our actual question is:

Is it supported in an IAS proxy system to assign a fixed, already existing IAS group purely inside the proxy transformation, even if the inbound payload does not contain groups?

Or is the only supported option in proxy mode one of these two?

  1. The calling system sends the group ID in the user payload.
  2. The user is assigned afterwards via a separate PATCH /Groups/{groupId} call.

Any confirmation, SAP guidance, or working example would be very helpful.

Thanks.

entry.title: A Look Under the Hood: SAP's Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI)
entry.content:
What is ABTI?

The Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI) is the connection point between SAP's Finance Business Transaction Interface (FIBTI) and Accounting. When a business transaction occurs, ABTI is the interface that receives that data and turns it into a real journal entry in Accounting.

FIBTI acts as the coordinator, collecting transaction data from sender applications and passing it along.

ABTI then takes over and processes that data and generates the journal entry in Accounting.

Why was ABTI developed?

A key motivation behind ABTI is decoupling.

First, operational and non‑Finance applications (such as Payroll or Real Estate) are decoupled from Finance‑specific logic. Through FIBTI, sender applications no longer need to deal with Accounting concerns like G/L account determination or valuation and can remain focused on their own business domain.
Second, ABTI introduces decoupling within Finance itself: business transactions received via FIBTI are handed over to Accounting independently, ensuring that Accounting-specific processing is clearly separated and centrally handled.

Previously, sender applications had to handle a lot of Accounting-specific logic themselves – including G/L account determination and currency valuation. ABTI moves all of that responsibility into Accounting, keeping sender applications focused on their own domain.

How does it work – at a high level?

ABTI's processing happens in two main phases:

  • Business transaction data is transformed into a preliminary journal entry. This logic differs depending on the transaction type, because a payroll posting looks very different from a bank fee, for example.
  • The preliminary journal entry is then processed further – G/L accounts are determined, currencies are converted, account assignments are derived, and period and budget checks are performed.

Good to know

Most users won't probably interact with ABTI directly. But knowing it exists, and what it does, can be useful when troubleshooting, configuring, or simply trying to understand how the pieces fit together. Especially because ABTI follows a posting logic that differs significantly from classic postings, it introduces new substitution concepts, and some classic BAdIs and substitutions are not processed in ABTI postings.

You can find more information on ABTI in the SAP Help Portal under Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI) | SAP Help Portal.

And if you are interested in more theory regarding the financial business transaction interface (FIBTI), you can read the Posting Logic of FIBTI: How SAP Finance Transactions Are Orchestrated - SAP Community blog or go to the SAP Help Portal under Finance Business Transaction Interface (FIBTI) | SAP Help Portal.

entry.title: Handling of Round-Off Values in SAP CAR and Integration with S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I’m looking for some clarification on how round-off values from POS transactions are handled within SAP Customer Activity Repository, particularly in scenarios involving invoice-level balancing.

Context:

  1. POS transactions include a round-off amount.
  2. In SAP CAR, I have the balancing of totals task active at the per-invoice level.
  3. This requires that the sum of line item totals matches the tender totals, including any round-off differences, to allow further aggregation.

Questions:

  1. How does SAP Customer Activity Repository process and store round-off values coming from POS?
  2. In cases where invoice-level balancing is enforced, what is the recommended approach to ensure line-item totals align with tender totals when round-off is involved?
  3. How are these round-off values transferred to SAP S/4HANA during financial posting?

Is there any standard configuration or best practice to handle round-off differences consistently across CAR and S/4HANA?
Any insights, configuration pointers, or examples would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Create supplier invoice app and import supplier invoi...
entry.content:
Good day @charlene2 

Thank you for your question.

That situation is usually caused by a difference in the source document type, not by the app name itself. In DRC, supplier invoice eDocument creation is controlled by which source document type is created in accounting and whether the source type is activated and mapped correctly for your company code. There are two separate mandatory configuration paths for supplier invoices. These are:

  1. Accounting Document source type: this requires you to activate Source Type Documents for Company Code and Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type.
  2. Invoice Verification source type: this requires assigning eDocument Type to Invoice Verification Transaction. You can use this when you want to create electronic invoices based on supplier invoices from the Create Supplier Invoice-Advanced app.

So, that distinction explains the result you received where:

  • Create Supplier Invoice-Advanced works because it follows the invoice Verification path, and that mapping is already in place.
  • Create Incoming Invoices works because the FI document type you posted is likely already mapped under Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type.
  • Create Supplier Invoice and Import Supplier Invoice creating pure FI supplier invoices do not flow to DRC most likely because the Accounting Document source type or the exact FI document type used by those postings is not activated or not assigned to an eDocument type.

So, if they are not generating eDocuments, the first thing you should check is whether the posted accounting document type from those two apps is assigned and whether Accounting Document is activated.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/8bdd8d920801423ca0f74c026854ebb1/3d515be6943a48b1a62f0013bfa3566f.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4977e7737cc747edb51d806619e3d146/38be92f5e67d40898db8f91273f7b12d.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Building a Production-Ready SAC Custom Widget with Claude AI — From Zero to Four Widgets
entry.content:
How I used Claude as an AI development partner to design and ship a full suite of Decomposition Tree story widgets for SAP Analytics Cloud 2026.8.0 — as a product manager with no coding background.

Why Custom Widgets Matter

SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Business Data Cloud give you something genuinely powerful: a rich, governed analytics platform with native planning, integrated SAP data, and a polished story-building experience. Most visualisation needs are covered out of the box — but when teams require something more specialised, the Custom Widget SDK becomes the extension point.

Build any chart type using any open source visualisation library, package it, upload it to the platform, and it appears in the story widget panel just like any native chart. Full data binding. Full styling panel. The same configuration experience story designers already know.

Best of both worlds: the governed, integrated platform your organisation relies on, extended with exactly the visualisation your team needs.

This post is about how I built a suite of Decomposition Tree story widgets using that SDK — and how I used Claude as an AI development partner to make it happen as a product manager with no development background.

What Is a Decomposition Tree Widget?

A decomposition tree is a left-to-right hierarchical visualisation for variance analysis. Each node represents a dimension member — a product category, a region, a sales manager — and shows its contribution as a horizontal bar against a plan or target. Branches connect parent nodes to their children, coloured to immediately signal over- or under-performance.

What makes this particularly useful for planning scenarios is the combination of drill flexibility and at-a-glance variance signalling. A viewer can start at the total, expand only the categories that are off-plan, drill into brands within those categories, and leave everything else collapsed. The widget respects the viewer's mental model rather than forcing them to see everything at once.

SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widgets — The Extension Point

The Custom Widget SDK is one of SAP Analytics Cloud's most powerful and underused capabilities. The idea is elegant: write a standard Web Component, describe its data bindings and configurable properties in a manifest file, package both as a zip, and upload to the platform. From that point on, the widget appears in the story widget panel alongside all native chart types.

Story designers bind it to data, configure its styling properties through a dedicated panel, and resize it like any other widget — all in the familiar story builder interface. The widget itself can use any JavaScript visualisation library: D3.js, ECharts, Chart.js, Highcharts, or anything else available from a CDN.

This is the best-of-both-worlds proposition: the governed, integrated SAP Analytics Cloud platform your organisation already relies on, with the freedom to deliver precisely the chart type your users need.

Getting Started: Requirements, Not Instructions

My starting point wasn't asking Claude to explain how custom widgets work. I didn't need to know. I described what I wanted — the visualisation, the features, the user experience — and gave Claude the reference material to work with: links to SAP's sample custom widget repository, relevant SAP Community blog posts, and the Custom Widget SDK documentation.

 
That was the pattern throughout the entire project. I stayed in product manager mode — describing outcomes, reacting to what I saw in SAP Analytics Cloud, and feeding Claude the context it needed. When something didn't look right, I'd paste in the error message or attach a screenshot. Claude would diagnose it and update the code.

Iterating Through Issues

Not everything worked first time — and when it didn't, the process was straightforward. Copy the error message from SAP Analytics Cloud, paste it into the conversation, attach a screenshot if it was a visual issue, and describe what I expected to see instead. Claude would identify the cause and produce an updated version of the relevant file.

Widget 1: The Foundation — Single Bar with Mini Charts

The first widget established the pattern every subsequent widget would build on. The goal: a horizontal bar per node coloured green or red by variance, with a dashed target tick, and mini bar charts showing the monthly breakdown inside each node.

Adding a Styling Panel

Once the core visualisation was working, I wanted story designers to be able to configure the widget's appearance directly in the story builder — colours, sizes, fonts, bar widths — without any code changes. I described what I needed: a panel that felt like the native chart styling options, with controls for every visual property, and dropdowns that reflected whichever dimensions the designer had bound to the widget.

The Custom Widget SDK supports exactly this through a dedicated styling panel — a separate component that appears alongside native chart styling options in the story builder. I gave Claude the requirements for each control and it built the panel, including the data binding that kept the dimension dropdowns in sync with whatever data was connected at the time. From a designer's perspective the result is seamless — configure the widget the same way you'd configure any native SAP Analytics Cloud chart.

The Widget Suite: An Iterative Journey

Each widget was built through conversation. I'd describe the user experience I wanted, Claude would generate the code and explain the key decisions, I'd validate it in SAP Analytics Cloud and refine the requirements based on what I saw, and we'd move to the next layer. Here's the progression:

 
Widgets 3 and 4: Refining the Experience
 
Widget 3 introduced the most requested feature: the ability to expand a single node without opening the entire level. Rather than clicking a category and seeing every brand underneath every other category expand at the same time, viewers could now explore just the one branch they cared about — everything else stayed as it was. It also introduced a dual-bar layout showing Actual and Plan as two separate bars per node for teams who wanted that explicit visual separation.
 
Widget 4 took the cleaner single-bar approach from Widget 1, added the per-node expand behaviour from Widget 3, and introduced one visual refinement I'd wanted from the start: branch lines that fade from the parent node's colour into the child node's colour. A small detail, but one that makes the variance story easier to read at a glance — you can trace a red thread through the hierarchy without having to read every label.
Both widgets went through the same process as Widget 1: describe the feature, validate in SAP Analytics Cloud, share what I saw, iterate. The conversation history from the earlier widgets meant Claude already understood the data model, the styling panel architecture, and the design decisions we'd already made — each new widget built directly on that shared context.

 

How I Actually Used Claude Day-to-Day

A few patterns emerged across the four widgets that made the collaboration work well:

1. Show Claude what you see, not what you think the fix is

When something didn't look or behave right, I didn't try to diagnose it myself — I just showed Claude what I was seeing. Sometimes that was a screenshot of the widget on the story canvas. Sometimes it was a console error I'd copied from the browser. Sometimes just a description: "the tooltip appears far below the cursor" or "clicking the expand button also opens the drill popup". Claude would identify the cause and update the code. As a non-developer, this is the approach that works: show the evidence, let Claude do the diagnosis.

2. Paste error messages exactly as they appear

Alongside screenshots, pasting the exact error text from SAP Analytics Cloud into the conversation was consistently the fastest path to a fix. The more precise the information — exact error text, a screenshot of the visual result, a description of what I expected instead — the faster we moved.

3. Build up a CLAUDE.md project knowledge file

As we confirmed what worked — the right packaging patterns, data binding conventions, rendering techniques — I asked Claude to help document each decision in a CLAUDE.md file in the project directory. By the end, this file captured every confirmed pattern, design choice, and platform behaviour we'd validated. Every new conversation started with Claude reading this context, so I never had to repeat or re-explain myself.

4. Incremental verification

I never asked Claude to write a full 800-line widget in one shot. We built it layer by layer: skeleton → data binding → tree layout → node rendering → styling panel → expand buttons. Each layer was tested in SAP Analytics Cloud before moving to the next.

5. Claude checks before you upload

Before packaging each new version, Claude would proactively review what it had built and flag anything worth addressing before testing in SAP Analytics Cloud — edge cases, timing issues, data states that only occur with certain model configurations, things that would only surface once the widget was live on a story canvas. I didn't have to ask.

Widget Suite at a Glance

You Don't Need to Know How It Works

One thing worth highlighting: I didn't need to understand the technical implementation to get here. The packaging format, the data binding conventions, the rendering patterns — Claude handled all of that. What I needed to understand was what I wanted the widget to do, how customers would want to use it, and what good looked like when I tested it in SAP Analytics Cloud.

If you're a fellow PM or business analyst wondering whether this is within reach — it is. The Custom Widget SDK is a developer tool, but with AI assistance you don't need to be the developer.

Conclusion

The widgets are running in our local tenants. Widget 4 (Decomposition Single Bar) is currently serving as the foundation for a production version our development team is building towards a beta release — the proof of concept gave us something concrete to react to and refine requirements against. Widget 3 (dual bar) remains available for use cases that benefit from the explicit visual separation of Actual and Plan.
 

One More Thing: Turning Everything We Learned into a Reusable Skill

By the end of this project, Claude had accumulated a deep working knowledge of the SAC Custom Widget SDK — every packaging rule, data binding quirk, cache busting step, and tooltip fix. That knowledge lived in a project file called CLAUDE.md that we built up iteratively throughout the work.
 
The natural next step was to distil that into a standalone Claude skill — a single file that captures everything needed to build a SAC custom widget from scratch. The idea is simple: instead of rediscovering the same gotchas on the next widget, you hand Claude the skill file at the start of a new project and it already knows everything. No trial and error, no re-explaining the packaging rules, no debugging the same cache issue twice.

 

The skill covers the full picture — index.json and main.js templates, data binding patterns, common errors and their fixes, the styling panel, linked analysis, and a plain-English guide for non-developers on what to tell Claude to get a working widget generated. Anyone can drop it into their own project and start building immediately.

It is something I am planning to share more broadly — both as a resource for others building on the Custom Widget SDK, and as part of the wider Custom Widget Marketplace initiative. If you would like a copy, reach out.

If you're considering building a custom widget for SAP Analytics Cloud, I hope this post gives you a practical picture of what's involved and what's achievable. The Custom Widget SDK is a genuinely powerful extension point — and with AI assistance, it's accessible even if you're coming at it from a product or business perspective rather than a development one.

 
Further reading
  • For the original SAP Custom Widget SDK guides, see series starting with: SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget – Hands-On Guide 
  • For more on AI-assisted SAP Analytics Cloud widget development, see the companion post by Adem Baykal: AI & Claude Code Experiment (Part 1): Building an SAP Analytics Cloud Widget

entry.title: Re: SAC: Scoping the date dimension using source version...
entry.content:
@Shailesh_mishra-983 

Currently you can only scope date based on version start and end property of a single version ( Target version parameter). So There are 2 option, either you use scripting to read the version attributes and assign the Data Action Parameter. Your User will have UX issues in this approach. The other way which it can work is you maintain an extra property say startdate2 in version dimension where the you keep start date of the source version as values this attribute startdate2  against Target member and then scope the date as below

MEMBERSET [d/Date] =[d/Version].[p/startdate2] TO [d/Version].[p/end]  // This will read property values of TargetVersion parameter

 

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

Nikhil
